label: Arithmetic & Number Operations
subject: algebra
common_errors: Integer division confusion (5/2 = 2 instead of 2.5)
Order of operations neglect (BODMAS/PEMDAS errors)
Sign errors when subtracting negative numbers
confusion_points: Difference between integer and real division
When to apply BODMAS vs left-to-right evaluation

label: Variables and Algebraic Expressions
subject: algebra
common_errors: Treating variables as fixed numbers
Not distributing multiplication over addition: a(b+c) = ab+c
Combining unlike terms: x + x² treated as 2x²
confusion_points: A variable represents any number, not a specific unknown
Like terms require the same variable AND same exponent

label: Linear Equations in One Variable
subject: algebra
common_errors: Not performing the same operation on both sides
Sign flip error when moving terms across the equals sign
Forgetting to distribute when expanding brackets
confusion_points: The goal is to isolate the variable, not simplify the expression
Moving a term across '=' changes its sign

label: Systems of Linear Equations
subject: algebra
common_errors: Adding equations incorrectly during elimination
Substituting back into the wrong original equation
Losing the second equation during substitution
confusion_points: When to use substitution vs elimination
A solution is a point (x, y) that satisfies BOTH equations simultaneously

label: Algebraic Inequalities
subject: algebra
common_errors: Not flipping the inequality sign when multiplying/dividing by a negative
Treating inequality solutions as single points instead of intervals
Forgetting to check endpoint inclusion for closed vs open intervals
confusion_points: Multiplying both sides by a negative flips the inequality direction
The solution set is a range, visualized on a number line

label: Exponents and Powers
subject: algebra
common_errors: a^m × a^n = a^(m×n) instead of a^(m+n)
(a+b)^2 = a^2 + b^2 (missing 2ab term)
Negative exponent means reciprocal, not negative result
confusion_points: Exponent rules only apply when the base is the same
a^0 = 1 for all non-zero a

label: Polynomials
subject: algebra
common_errors: Adding polynomials without aligning like terms
Multiplying polynomials by only multiplying first terms
Forgetting to include the negative sign when subtracting polynomials
confusion_points: Degree of a polynomial is the highest power of the variable
Every term in one polynomial must multiply every term in the other

label: Factoring Polynomials
subject: algebra
common_errors: Forgetting to factor out the GCF first
Sign errors in factor pairs: finding wrong pairs that sum to middle term
Not checking factored form by expanding back
confusion_points: Factoring is the reverse of expanding
Always check for a common factor before using other techniques

label: Quadratic Equations
subject: algebra
common_errors: Forgetting to set each factor equal to zero
Quadratic formula sign errors under the radical
Not checking for extraneous solutions when squaring
confusion_points: A quadratic can have 0, 1, or 2 real solutions
Discriminant b²-4ac determines the number of real roots

label: Logarithms
subject: algebra
common_errors: log(a+b) treated as log(a) + log(b)
log(ab) = log(a) + log(b) confused with log(a^b) = b·log(a)
Forgetting the domain restriction: log is undefined for non-positive arguments
confusion_points: log_b(x) = y means b^y = x — logarithm is the inverse of exponentiation
Change of base formula: log_b(x) = ln(x)/ln(b)

label: Functions and Function Notation
subject: algebra
common_errors: f(x+h) expanded incorrectly as f(x) + f(h)
Confusing the domain (inputs) and range (outputs)
Treating f(x) as f multiplied by x
confusion_points: f(x) is a rule, not a product
A function assigns exactly one output to each input

label: Rational Expressions
subject: algebra
common_errors: Cancelling terms instead of factors: (x²+1)/(x+1) ≠ x
Forgetting to exclude values that make the denominator zero
Adding fractions without finding common denominator first
confusion_points: Only common factors (not terms) can be cancelled
Always state the domain restrictions

label: Angles and Radian Measure
subject: trigonometry
common_errors: Mixing degrees and radians in the same calculation
Forgetting that 2π radians = 360 degrees
Using degree-mode calculator for radian problems
confusion_points: Radians are dimensionless ratios, not 'units' in the usual sense
π radians = 180 degrees is the core conversion fact

label: Pythagorean Theorem
subject: trigonometry
common_errors: Applying to non-right triangles
Confusing hypotenuse with a leg: a² + c² = b² when b is hypotenuse
Arithmetic error when taking the square root
confusion_points: The theorem applies ONLY to right triangles
The hypotenuse is always the side opposite the right angle

label: Basic Trigonometric Ratios (SOH-CAH-TOA)
subject: trigonometry
common_errors: Confusing sin and cos definitions (opposite vs adjacent)
Using the wrong side as hypotenuse
Forgetting that trig ratios depend on which angle is the reference
confusion_points: SOH: sin = Opposite/Hypotenuse, CAH: cos = Adjacent/Hypotenuse, TOA: tan = Opposite/Adjacent
Always specify which angle you are referencing

label: Unit Circle
subject: trigonometry
common_errors: Misremembering quadrant signs for sin/cos/tan
Confusing reference angle with the actual angle
Mixing up coordinates: cos is x-coordinate, sin is y-coordinate
confusion_points: On the unit circle, (x,y) = (cos θ, sin θ)
ASTC mnemonic: All Sine Tangent Cosine — which are positive per quadrant

label: Trigonometric Identities
subject: trigonometry
common_errors: Using sin²x + cos²x = 1 with wrong squaring: sin(x)² ≠ sin(x²)
Applying sum formula with wrong sign: cos(A+B) ≠ cosA·cosB + sinA·sinB
Confusing double angle and half angle formulas
confusion_points: Pythagorean identity: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 is foundational
Sum formulas must be memorized carefully — each has specific signs

label: Inverse Trigonometric Functions
subject: trigonometry
common_errors: Confusing sin⁻¹(x) with 1/sin(x)
Ignoring restricted domains: arcsin range is [-π/2, π/2]
Not accounting for multiple solutions outside principal range
confusion_points: arcsin is NOT the reciprocal of sin; it is the inverse function
Inverse trig functions have restricted ranges to make them proper functions

label: Law of Sines
subject: trigonometry
common_errors: Forgetting the ambiguous case (SSA can give 0, 1, or 2 triangles)
Using degrees when calculator is in radian mode
Applying to right triangles when simpler methods suffice
confusion_points: a/sin(A) = b/sin(B) = c/sin(C) — each side paired with its opposite angle
The ambiguous case arises when the given angle is acute

label: Law of Cosines
subject: trigonometry
common_errors: Forgetting the -2bc·cos(A) term
Using wrong angle: angle A is opposite side a
Not simplifying to Pythagorean theorem when angle is 90°
confusion_points: c² = a² + b² - 2ab·cos(C) generalises the Pythagorean theorem
When C = 90°, cos(90°) = 0 and the formula reduces to a² + b² = c²

label: Limits
subject: calculus
common_errors: Plugging in the limit point directly without checking if the form is 0/0
Confusing the limit with the function value at the point
Forgetting one-sided limits for piecewise functions
confusion_points: lim_{x→a} f(x) asks about behavior NEAR a, not AT a
0/0 is an indeterminate form that requires further analysis

label: Continuity
subject: calculus
common_errors: Confusing continuity with differentiability
Forgetting to check all three conditions: limit exists, f(a) exists, they're equal
Assuming continuous functions are differentiable everywhere
confusion_points: A function is continuous at a if lim_{x→a} f(x) = f(a)
Differentiability implies continuity but not vice versa

label: Derivatives and Differentiation
subject: calculus
common_errors: Power rule error: d/dx(x^n) = x^(n-1) instead of n·x^(n-1)
Differentiating the constant inside trig: d/dx(sin(2x)) = cos(2x) not 2cos(2x)
Forgetting the chain rule for composite functions
confusion_points: The derivative is the instantaneous rate of change (slope of tangent)
dy/dx is not a fraction but behaves like one in many useful ways

label: Chain Rule
subject: calculus
common_errors: Forgetting to differentiate the inner function
Applying the chain rule when the function is not composite
Misidentifying the outer and inner functions in f(g(x))
confusion_points: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) · g'(x) — derivative of outer times derivative of inner
Always ask: is there a function of a function here?

label: Product Rule
subject: calculus
common_errors: d/dx(f·g) = f'·g' instead of f'·g + f·g'
Applying product rule when one factor is a constant
Forgetting to apply product rule to three-factor products
confusion_points: d/dx(u·v) = u'v + uv' — each factor differentiated once while the other remains
Constants have derivative zero, so product rule simplifies

label: Quotient Rule
subject: calculus
common_errors: Reversing numerator subtraction: (g·f' - f·g')/g² instead of (f'g - fg')/g²
Forgetting to square the denominator
Using quotient rule when rewriting as product is simpler
confusion_points: d/dx(f/g) = (f'g - fg') / g² — 'low d-high minus high d-low, over low squared'
The denominator must be squared, not just multiplied

label: Implicit Differentiation
subject: calculus
common_errors: Forgetting to apply chain rule when differentiating y terms (adding dy/dx)
Treating y as a constant instead of a function of x
Not collecting dy/dx terms on one side
confusion_points: d/dx(y²) = 2y · dy/dx — chain rule applies because y depends on x
After differentiating, collect all dy/dx terms and solve algebraically

label: Related Rates
subject: calculus
common_errors: Substituting given values BEFORE differentiating with respect to time
Not identifying all variables changing with time
Forgetting to include units in the final answer
confusion_points: All variable quantities are functions of time t
Differentiate the geometric relationship implicitly with respect to t

label: Optimization (Maxima and Minima)
subject: calculus
common_errors: Finding critical points but not testing whether they are max or min
Ignoring endpoint values for closed interval optimization
Setting the original function equal to zero instead of its derivative
confusion_points: Set f'(x) = 0 to find critical points; use second derivative test to classify
Always check endpoints when the domain is a closed interval

label: Integration (Indefinite and Definite)
subject: calculus
common_errors: Forgetting the constant of integration C for indefinite integrals
Integrating as if it were differentiation: ∫x² dx = x³ not x³/3
Swapping limits of integration without negating
confusion_points: Integration is the reverse of differentiation — find a function whose derivative is given
Definite integral gives area; indefinite integral gives a family of functions

label: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
subject: calculus
common_errors: Forgetting to evaluate the antiderivative at BOTH limits
Reversing the subtraction: F(a) - F(b) instead of F(b) - F(a)
Differentiating the integral incorrectly when the upper limit is a function
confusion_points: ∫[a to b] f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a) where F is any antiderivative of f
d/dx[∫[a to x] f(t)dt] = f(x) — differentiation undoes integration

label: Integration by Parts
subject: calculus
common_errors: Wrong choice of u and dv (LIATE rule helps: Log, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trig, Exponential)
Forgetting to integrate dv completely before applying the formula
Not recognising when to apply the formula twice
confusion_points: ∫u dv = uv - ∫v du — choose u to be the function that simplifies when differentiated
LIATE gives the priority order for choosing u

label: Integration by Substitution (u-substitution)
subject: calculus
common_errors: Forgetting to substitute dx in terms of du
Not changing the limits of integration for definite integrals
Choosing u as a constant or something that doesn't simplify the integral
confusion_points: If u = g(x) then du = g'(x)dx — rewrite everything in terms of u
For definite integrals, change x-limits to u-limits or substitute back before evaluating

label: Vectors
subject: physics
common_errors: Adding magnitudes instead of component-wise: |A+B| ≠ |A| + |B|
Confusing vector and scalar quantities
Wrong angle reference when resolving components
confusion_points: Vectors have both magnitude AND direction; scalars have only magnitude
Always resolve into x and y components before adding

label: Kinematics (Motion in 1D and 2D)
subject: physics
common_errors: Using v = u + at when acceleration is not constant
Confusing displacement (vector) with distance (scalar)
Wrong sign convention: choosing up as positive but applying it inconsistently
confusion_points: The five kinematic equations only apply for constant acceleration
Displacement is the area under a v-t graph; acceleration is the slope

label: Newton's First Law (Inertia)
subject: physics
common_errors: Believing a moving object needs a force to keep moving
Ignoring friction as the force that causes deceleration
Confusing inertia with mass
confusion_points: Objects in motion stay in motion unless acted on by a net external force
Zero net force means constant velocity, not zero velocity

label: Newton's Second Law (F = ma)
subject: physics
common_errors: Using total mass instead of the object's mass when multiple objects are present
Adding force magnitudes without accounting for directions
Forgetting to include all forces in the free body diagram
confusion_points: F_net = ma — it is the NET (resultant) force, not any single force
Draw a free body diagram to identify all forces before summing

label: Newton's Third Law (Action-Reaction)
subject: physics
common_errors: Treating action-reaction pairs as cancelling each other (they act on DIFFERENT objects)
Confusing Newton's 3rd law pairs with balanced forces
Applying action-reaction to forces on the same body
confusion_points: Action and reaction are equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, on DIFFERENT bodies
They do not cancel because they act on different objects

label: Work, Energy, and Power
subject: physics
common_errors: Work = F × d only when F is parallel to d (must use F·d·cosθ generally)
Confusing kinetic energy (½mv²) with momentum (mv)
Forgetting that work done by friction is always negative
confusion_points: Work = F·d·cosθ where θ is the angle between force and displacement
Work-energy theorem: W_net = ΔKE

label: Momentum and Impulse
subject: physics
common_errors: Ignoring direction in collision problems (momentum is a vector)
Confusing elastic and inelastic collision conditions
Applying conservation of momentum when external forces are present
confusion_points: Momentum p = mv is conserved when net external force is zero
Impulse = F·Δt = Δp — force applied over time changes momentum

label: Circular Motion
subject: physics
common_errors: Centripetal force is not a new force — it is the net inward force
Using wrong formula: ac = v²/r not v/r or v²·r
Forgetting that speed is constant in uniform circular motion but velocity is not
confusion_points: Centripetal acceleration = v²/r always points toward the centre
Any force providing centripetal acceleration (tension, gravity, normal) depends on context

label: Gravitation
subject: physics
common_errors: Using g = 9.8 m/s² at heights where it no longer applies
Confusing gravitational force with weight (W = mg is weight, F = Gm₁m₂/r² is gravitational)
Forgetting that r is the distance from the centre of mass, not the surface
confusion_points: Universal law: F = Gm₁m₂/r² — force between any two masses
Orbital mechanics: gravitational force provides centripetal force

label: Atomic Structure
subject: chemistry
common_errors: Confusing atomic number (protons) with mass number (protons + neutrons)
Placing electrons without following Aufbau, Pauli exclusion, Hund's rules
Confusing isotopes (same protons, different neutrons) with isobars
confusion_points: Atomic number = number of protons (defines the element)
Electron configuration determines chemical properties

label: Periodic Table and Trends
subject: chemistry
common_errors: Electronegativity trend: wrongly thinking it increases down a group
Atomic radius trend: confusing period (decreases left to right) and group trends
Ionization energy exceptions: N > O due to half-filled p subshell stability
confusion_points: Periodic trends arise from nuclear charge and shielding effects
Exceptions exist (e.g. IP: N > O) due to subshell stability

label: Chemical Bonding
subject: chemistry
common_errors: Forgetting lone pairs when drawing Lewis structures
Confusing sigma and pi bonds in multiple bonds
Wrong VSEPR geometry when lone pairs are present
confusion_points: Octet rule: atoms bond to achieve 8 valence electrons (H needs 2)
Lone pairs on central atom distort geometry relative to electron geometry

label: Mole Concept and Avogadro's Number
subject: chemistry
common_errors: Confusing moles with mass: n = m/M not n = m
Using Avogadro's number incorrectly for moles of molecules vs atoms
Forgetting to use molar mass (g/mol) not atomic mass (amu) for bulk calculations
confusion_points: 1 mole = 6.022 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's number)
Molar mass numerically equals atomic/molecular weight in g/mol

label: Chemical Equations and Balancing
subject: chemistry
common_errors: Changing subscripts instead of coefficients to balance
Not balancing polyatomic ions as a unit when they appear on both sides
Forgetting to check if all atoms AND charges are balanced in ionic equations
confusion_points: Balance by adjusting coefficients ONLY — subscripts define the compound
Check all elements and charges after balancing

label: Stoichiometry
subject: chemistry
common_errors: Not converting grams to moles before using mole ratios
Using the wrong mole ratio from the equation
Forgetting to identify the limiting reagent in excess reagent problems
confusion_points: Mole ratio from balanced equation = conversion factor between reactants/products
Limiting reagent determines the maximum product formed

label: Acids, Bases, and pH
subject: chemistry
common_errors: pH = -log[H+] but students use log[H+] without the negative sign
Confusing strong/weak acid with concentrated/dilute acid
Forgetting that pH + pOH = 14 at 25°C only
confusion_points: Strong acid/base fully dissociates; weak acid/base partially dissociates
pH = -log[H+]; a lower pH means MORE acidic (higher [H+])

label: Thermochemistry and Hess's Law
subject: chemistry
common_errors: Forgetting to reverse the sign of ΔH when reversing a reaction
Not multiplying ΔH by stoichiometric coefficients when scaling a reaction
Confusing ΔH (enthalpy) with internal energy ΔU
confusion_points: Hess's law: ΔH is path-independent — add reactions to get target reaction
Exothermic: ΔH < 0 (system loses heat); endothermic: ΔH > 0
